title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Overview of the Union Budget 2026
The Union Budget 2026–27 was presented by Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman in the Lok Sabha on 1 February 2026. Every year, the budget acts like a financial roadmap for the country, explaining how the government plans to use public money and what changes citizens can expect in the coming months.
The year’s budget focuses on infrastructural development to boost industrial and economic growth while also paying attention to people’s needs, such as jobs, education, healthcare, and financial safety. These decisions matter because they influence everyday costs, savings habits, and how you plan for your future.
Six Key Pillars of Budget 2026–27
The Union Budget 2026–27 follows a practical approach that focuses on taking action, making meaningful reforms, and keeping people at the centre of growth. This approach is supported by six key pillars
Sustaining Economic Growth
Strengthening the Foundations of Growth
People-Centric Development
Trust-Based Governance
Ease of Doing Business and Ease of Living,
Fiscal matters
Sector-wise Reforms in Union Budget 2026–27
Manufacturing and Industrial Sector
Manufacturing remains a core focus of the budget, with special attention on high-value and technology-driven industries. The government has announced multiple schemes to strengthen domestic production and reduce reliance on imports.
Key reforms include:
A five-year income tax exemption will be available for non-residents who supply capital goods, machinery, or tooling to toll manufacturers operating in bonded zones.
Non-residents using bonded warehouses for component storage will be covered under safe harbour provisions
Trusted manufacturers will be allowed to defer duty payments, easing short-term cash flow pressure.
The duty-free import limit for inputs used in seafood processing for exports has been increased from 1% to 3% of the previous year’s FOB export value.
Duty-free import benefits for specified inputs have been extended to cover shoe uppers, including leather and synthetic footwear exports.
Export timelines for leather and textile garments as well as synthetic footwear have been extended from six months to one year, providing greater operational flexibility to exporters.
Selected parts used in the manufacture of microwave ovens will now be exempt from basic customs duty.
Components and parts required for aircraft manufacturing will be exempt from basic customs duty.
Raw materials imported for manufacturing aircraft parts used in defence-related maintenance, repair, and overhaul activities will also attract customs duty exemption.
Importers with established and reliable supply chains will be recognised as trusted entities within the risk management system reducing the need for verification every time.
Export consignments using electronic sealing will be allowed direct clearance from factory premises to ports, reducing handling delays
A one-time concessional duty window will be provided to eligible SEZ manufacturing units to sell goods in the domestic tariff area.
MSME Sector
Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) are supported through a three-pronged approach focused on equity, liquidity, and professional support.
Major reforms include:
Provision of a ₹10,000 crore SME Growth Fund
Additional ₹2,000 crore support to the Self-Reliant India Fund
Mandatory use of TReDS for CPSE purchases from MSMEs to improve payment cycles
Credit guarantee support for invoice discounting
Linking GeM with TReDS for faster and cheaper financing
Removal of the ₹10 lakh cap on courier exports
Service Sector
The services sector continues to be recognised as a key driver of economic growth and employment. Recognising the impact of the service sector, the government has proposed a High-Powered ‘Education to Employment and Enterprise’ Standing Committee, which will focus on aligning education, skills, and entrepreneurship with the evolving needs of the services economy. Apart from they have also introduced reforms in different industries of service, which are highlighted below.
Healthcare and Allied Health Services
With rising demand for health care and allied services, the budget aims to meet the demand for skilled workers with the following initiatives -
Upgrading existing institutions and setting up new ones for Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) across ten selected disciplines
Development of NSQF-aligned training programmes to train 1.5 lakh multiskilled caregivers.
Support to states for establishing five Medical Value Tourism hubs in partnership with the private sector.
The AYUSH initiative will be strengthened through the establishment of three new All India Institutes of Ayurveda, along with upgrades to AYUSH pharmacies, drug testing laboratories, and the WHO Global Traditional Medicine Centre to improve certification standards.
Education and sports
Setting up five University Townships near major industrial and logistics corridors
Construction of girls’ hostels in higher education STEM institutions in every district
Setting up or upgrading four telescope infrastructure facilities to support advanced research
Setting up AVGC Content Creator Labs in 15,000 secondary schools and 500 colleges to support the orange (creative)economy
Establishment of a new National Institute of Design in the eastern region of India through the challenge route
Strengthening the Khelo India Mission through structured talent development with systematic coaching, technology integration, and sports infrastructure development.
Tourism
Tourism services receive a strong boost in the budget 2026, with emphasis on sustainability, heritage conservation, and experience-based travel.
Key initiatives include:
Creation of a National Destination Digital Knowledge Grid to digitally document places of national importance
Development of sustainable mountain treks, bird-watching trails, and turtle trails in specified states
Development of 15 archaeological sites into experiential cultural destinations
India is hosting the first-ever Global Big Cat Summit
Development of Buddhist circuits in the North East region.
Financial Services
Setting up a High-Level Committee on Banking aligned with India’s next growth phase
Incentive of ₹100 crore for single municipal bond issuances exceeding ₹1,000 crore
Restructuring of Power Finance Corporation (PFC) and Rural Electrification Corporation (REC)
Comprehensive review of FEMA (Non-debt Instruments) Rules
Introduction of market-making frameworks and total return swaps on corporate bonds
Increase in Securities Transaction Tax (STT) on futures from 0.02% to 0.05%
The Securities Transaction Tax (STT) on options premiums and the exercise of options will increase to 0.15%, from the current rates of 0.1% and 0.125%, respectively.
IT Department
Key reforms include:
Grouping IT services under a single category with a common safe harbour margin of 15.5%
Increase in the safe harbour threshold from ₹300 crore to ₹2,000 crore
Automated, rule-based approval of safe harbour
Option to continue safe harbour for five years at the company’s choice
Faster unilateral APA process with a target timeline of two years, extendable by six months
Extension of modified return facilities to associated entities of APA-availing companies
Tax holidays until 2047 for foreign companies providing cloud services through India-based data centres
Safe harbour of 15% on cost for related data centre service entities
Exemption on the global income of non-resident experts for a stay of up to five years under the notified schemes.
4. Agriculture and Allied Activities
Farmers are considered the backbone of the country. To support farming and allied government plans to introduce many reforms in different agro-related activities
Key Focus Areas and Initiatives
Horticulture and High-Value Crops
A dedicated programme for horticulture will rejuvenate old, low-yield orchards.
Animal Husbandry
Support for entrepreneurship in animal husbandry to create jobs in rural and peri-urban areas.
Loan-linked capital subsidy schemes to help set up veterinary colleges, hospitals, diagnostic labs, and breeding facilities in the private sector.
Supporting High-Value Agriculture
Dedicated programmes for Indian cashew and cocoa to improve cultivation and processing.
Coconut Promotion Scheme to increase production and enhance productivity.
Fisheries
Integrated development of 500 reservoirs and Amrit Sarovars.
Strengthening fisheries value chains in coastal areas.
Improving market linkages for start-ups and women-led groups through Fish FPOs.
Sandalwood
Focused cultivation and post-harvest processing of sandalwood to support high-value forestry-based income.
Bharat-VISTAAR and Technology Integration
Integration of AgriStack portals with the ICAR package of agricultural practices.
Use of AI-enabled systems to support smarter, data-driven farming decisions.
Important Policy and Procedural Changes Introduced in Budget 2026–27
Persons resident outside India will now be allowed to invest in equity shares of listed Indian companies through the Portfolio Investment Scheme (PIS).
Interest compensation awarded by a Motor Accident Claims Tribunal to an individual will be fully exempt from income tax, and no TDS will apply on such payments.
The TCS rate on overseas tour packages has been reduced to 2%, replacing the earlier rates of 5% and 20%, with no minimum amount condition.
TCS under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme for education and medical expenses has been lowered from 5% to 2%.
TDS on manpower supply services will be applied at a reduced rate of 1% or 2%, depending on the nature of the transaction.
Small taxpayers will be able to obtain lower or nil TDS deduction certificates through an automated, rule-based system.
Depositories will be permitted to accept Form 15G and Form 15H even when taxpayers hold securities across multiple companies.
The deadline for revising income tax returns has been extended from 31 December to 31 March, subject to payment of a nominal fee.
Individuals filing ITR-1 and ITR-2 will continue to have a due date of 31 July, while non-audit business cases and trusts will be allowed time up to 31 August.
For the sale of immovable property by a non-resident, TDS will now be deducted and deposited using the resident buyer’s PAN, removing the need for a TAN.
A one-time, six-month window will be introduced for disclosure of foreign assets below a specified threshold for small taxpayers.
Taxpayers will be allowed to update their returns even after reassessment proceedings begin by paying an additional 10% tax over the applicable rate.
The framework granting immunity from penalty and prosecution in cases of underreporting will now also apply to certain cases of misreporting.
Failure to maintain books of accounts or delay in TDS payment will be decriminalised, reducing prosecution-related burden.
Retrospective immunity from prosecution (effective 1 October 2024) will be provided for non-disclosure of foreign assets other than immovable property, where the total value is below ₹20 lakh.
Non-residents opting for presumptive taxation will be exempt from Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT).
A joint committee of the Ministry of Corporate Affairs and CBDT will be formed to align Income Computation and Disclosure Standards (ICDS) with Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS).
Share buybacks will be taxed as capital gains for all shareholders, while promoters will be subject to an additional buyback tax.
Set-off of available MAT credit will be allowed up to 25% of the tax liability under the new tax regime.
MAT is proposed to be treated as a final tax, simplifying compliance.
Basic Customs Duty will be exempted on 17 specified cancer-related drugs and medicines.
A single, integrated digital window will be introduced for cargo clearance approvals.
The Customs Integrated System (CIS) will be implemented over the next two years.
Taxpayers who wish to settle disputes amicably will be allowed to close cases by paying an additional amount instead of facing penalties.
Income Tax Reforms Introduced in Budget 2026-27
The Income Tax Act, 1961 had been in place for more than 60 years. With numerous amendments added across decades, the law had grown outdated and confusing for many taxpayers, making compliance harder than it needed to be.
To address this, the Union Budget 2026–27 introduces the Income Tax Act, 2025, which will come into force from 1 April 2026 as announced by finance minister today. The new law aims to simplify the tax system by simplifying and introducing changes in today’s modern era. At the same time, the government has kept income tax slabs unchanged t and extended the deadline to revise Income Tax Returns up to 31 March of the assessment year, giving taxpayers more time to make corrections
Conclusion
While the budget does not announce any reforms related to life insurance or introduce changes to life insurance taxation, life insurance and health insurance policies continue to attract 0% GST, keeping protection affordable for families.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
Sources for all other info except for the linked ones -https://www.indiabudget.gov.in/doc/bh1.pdf
full_pdf_text:
Overview of the Union Budget 2026
The Union Budget 2026–27 was presented in the Lok Sabha on 1 February 2026. Every year, the budget acts like a financial roadmap for the country, explaining how the government plans to use public money and what changes citizens can expect in the coming months.
The year’s budget focuses on infrastructural development to boost industrial and economic growth while also paying attention to people’s needs, such as jobs, education, healthcare, and financial safety. These decisions matter because they influence everyday costs, savings habits, and how you plan for your future.
Six Key Pillars of Budget 2026–27
The Union Budget 2026–27 follows a practical approach that focuses on taking action, making meaningful reforms, and keeping people at the centre of growth. This approach is supported by six key pillars1
1. Sustaining Economic Growth 2. Strengthening the Foundations of Growth 3. People-Centric Development 4. Trust-Based Governance 5. Ease of Doing Business and Ease of Living, 6. Fiscal matters
Sector-wise Reforms in Union Budget 2026–27*
1. Manufacturing and Industrial Sector1
Manufacturing remains a core focus of the budget, with special attention on high-value and technology-driven industries. The government has announced multiple schemes to strengthen domestic production and reduce reliance on imports.
Key reforms include:
● A five-year income tax exemption will be available for non-residents who supply capital goods, machinery, or tooling to toll manufacturers operating in bonded zones. ● Non-residents using bonded warehouses for component storage will be covered under safe harbour provisions ● Trusted manufacturers will be allowed to defer duty payments, easing short-term cash flow pressure. ● The duty-free import limit for inputs used in seafood processing for exports has been increased from 1% to 3% of the previous year’s Free On Board export value. ● Duty-free import benefits for specified inputs have been extended to cover shoe uppers, including leather and synthetic footwear exports. ● Export timelines for leather and textile garments as well as synthetic footwear have been extended from six months to one year, providing greater operational flexibility to exporters.
● Selected parts used in the manufacture of microwave ovens will now be exempt from basic customs duty. ● Components and parts required for aircraft manufacturing will be exempt from basic customs duty. ● Raw materials imported for manufacturing aircraft parts used in defence-related maintenance, repair, and overhaul activities will also attract customs duty exemption. ● Importers with established and reliable supply chains will be recognised within the risk management system. ● Export consignments using electronic sealing will be allowed direct clearance from factory premises to ports, reducing handling delays ● A one-time concessional duty window will be provided to eligible SEZ manufacturing units to sell goods in the domestic tariff area.
2. MSME Sector1
Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) are supported through a three-pronged approach focused on equity, liquidity, and professional support.
Major reforms include:
● Provision of a ₹10,000 crore SME Growth Fund ● Additional ₹2,000 crore support to the Self-Reliant India Fund ● Mandatory use of TReDS for CPSE purchases from MSMEs to improve payment cycles ● Credit guarantee support for invoice discounting ● Linking GeM with TReDS for faster and cheaper financing ● Removal of the ₹10 lakh cap on courier exports
3. Service Sector1
The services sector continues to be recognised as a key driver of economic growth and employment. Recognising the impact of the service sector, the government has proposed a High-Powered ‘Education to Employment and Enterprise’ Standing Committee, which will focus on aligning education, skills, and entrepreneurship with the evolving needs of the services economy. Apart from they have also introduced reforms in different industries of service, which are highlighted below.
A. Healthcare and Allied Health Services1
With rising demand for health care and allied services, the budget aims to meet the demand for skilled workers with the following initiatives -
● Upgrading existing institutions and setting up new ones for Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) across ten selected disciplines ● Development of NSQF-aligned training programmes to train 1.5 lakh multiskilled caregivers. ● Support to states for establishing five Medical Value Tourism hubs in partnership with the private sector.
● The AYUSH initiative will be strengthened through the establishment of three new All India Institutes of Ayurveda, along with upgrades to AYUSH pharmacies, drug testing laboratories, and the WHO Global Traditional Medicine Centre to improve certification standards. B. Education and sports1
● Setting up five University Townships near major industrial and logistics corridors ● Construction of girls’ hostels in higher education STEM institutions in every district ● Setting up or upgrading four telescope infrastructure facilities to support advanced research ● Setting up AVGC Content Creator Labs in 15,000 secondary schools and 500 colleges to support the orange (creative)economy ● Establishment of a new National Institute of Design in the eastern region of India through the challenge route ● Strengthening the Khelo India Mission through structured talent development with systematic coaching, technology integration, and sports infrastructure development. C. Tourism1
Tourism services receive a strong boost in the budget 2026, with emphasis on sustainability, heritage conservation, and experience-based travel.
Key initiatives include:
● Creation of a National Destination Digital Knowledge Grid to digitally document places of national importance ● Development of sustainable mountain treks, bird-watching trails, and turtle trails in specified states ● Development of 15 archaeological sites into experiential cultural destinations ● India is hosting the first-ever Global Big Cat Summit ● Development of Buddhist circuits in the North East region. D. Financial Services1
● Setting up a High-Level Committee on Banking aligned with India’s next growth phase ● Incentive of ₹100 crore for single municipal bond issuances exceeding ₹1,000 crore ● Restructuring of Power Finance Corporation (PFC) and Rural Electrification Corporation (REC) ● Comprehensive review of FEMA (Non-debt Instruments) Rules ● Introduction of market-making frameworks and total return swaps on corporate bonds ● Increase in Securities Transaction Tax (STT) on futures from 0.02% to 0.05% ● The Securities Transaction Tax (STT) on options premiums and the exercise of options will increase to 0.15%, from the current rates of 0.1% and 0.125%,
respectively. E. IT Department1
Key reforms include:
● Grouping IT services under a single category with a common safe harbour margin of 15.5% ● Increase in the safe harbour threshold from ₹300 crore to ₹2,000 crore ● Automated, rule-based approval of safe harbour ● Option to continue safe harbour for five years at the company’s choice ● Faster unilateral APA process with a target timeline of two years, extendable by six months on the tax payer’s request. ● Extension of modified return facilities to associated entities of APA-availing companies ● Tax holidays until 2047 for foreign companies providing cloud services through India- based data centres ● Safe harbour of 15% on cost for related data centre service entities ● Exemption on the global income of non-resident experts for a stay of up to five years under the notified schemes.
4. Agriculture and Allied Activities1
Farmers are considered the backbone of the country. To support farming and allied government plans to introduce many reforms in different agro-related activities
Key Focus Areas and Initiatives
Horticulture and High-Value Crops
● A dedicated programme for horticulture will rejuvenate old, low-yield orchards.
● Expand high-density cultivation of walnuts, almonds, pine nuts
Animal Husbandry
● Support for entrepreneurship in animal husbandry to create jobs in rural and peri-urban areas. ● Loan-linked capital subsidy schemes to help set up veterinary colleges, hospitals, diagnostic labs, and breeding facilities in the private sector.
Supporting High-Value Agriculture
● Dedicated programmes for Indian cashew and cocoa to improve cultivation and processing. ● Coconut Promotion Scheme to increase production and enhance productivity.
Fisheries1
● Integrated development of 500 reservoirs and Amrit Sarovars. ● Strengthening fisheries value chains in coastal areas. ● Improving market linkages for start-ups and women-led groups through Fish FPOs.
Sandalwood1
● Focused cultivation and post-harvest processing of sandalwood to support high-value forestry-based income.
Bharat-VISTAAR and Technology Integration1
● Integration of AgriStack portals with the ICAR package of agricultural practices. ● Use of AI-enabled systems to support smarter, data-driven farming decisions.
Important Policy and Procedural Changes Introduced in Budget 2026–271
● Persons resident outside India will now be allowed to invest in equity shares of listed Indian companies through the Portfolio Investment Scheme (PIS). ● Interest compensation awarded by a Motor Accident Claims Tribunal to an individual will be fully exempt from income tax, and no TDS will apply on such payments. ● The TCS rate on overseas tour packages has been reduced to 2%, replacing the earlier rates of 5% and 20%, with no minimum amount condition. ● TCS under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme for education and medical expenses has been lowered from 5% to 2%. ● TDS on manpower supply services will be applied at a reduced rate of 1% or 2%, depending on the nature of the transaction. ● Small taxpayers will be able to obtain lower or nil TDS deduction certificates through an automated, rule-based system. ● Depositories will be permitted to accept Form 15G and Form 15H even when taxpayers hold securities across multiple companies. ● The deadline for revising income tax returns has been extended from 31 December to 31 March, subject to payment of a nominal fee. ● Individuals filing ITR-1 and ITR-2 will continue to have a due date of 31 July, while non-audit business cases and trusts will be allowed time up to 31 August. ● For the sale of immovable property by a non-resident, TDS will now be deducted and deposited using the resident buyer’s PAN, removing the need for a TAN. ● A one-time, six-month window will be introduced for disclosure of foreign assets below a specified threshold for small taxpayers. ● Taxpayers will be allowed to update their returns even after reassessment proceedings begin by paying an additional 10% tax over the applicable rate. ● The framework granting immunity from penalty and prosecution in cases of underreporting will now also apply to certain cases of misreporting. ● Failure to maintain books of accounts or delay in TDS payment will be decriminalised, reducing prosecution-related burden.
● Retrospective immunity from prosecution (effective 1 October 2024) will be provided for non-disclosure of foreign assets other than immovable property, where the total value is below ₹20 lakh. ● Non-residents opting for presumptive taxation will be exempt from Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT). ● A joint committee of the Ministry of Corporate Affairs and CBDT will be formed to align Income Computation and Disclosure Standards (ICDS) with Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS). ● Share buybacks will be taxed as capital gains for all shareholders, while promoters will be subject to an additional buyback tax. ● Set-off of available MAT credit will be allowed up to 25% of the tax liability under the new tax regime. ● MAT is proposed to be treated as a final tax, simplifying compliance. ● Basic Customs Duty will be exempted on 17 specified cancer-related drugs and medicines. ● A single, integrated digital window will be introduced for cargo clearance approvals. ● The Customs Integrated System (CIS) will be implemented over the next two years. ● Taxpayers who wish to settle disputes amicably will be allowed to close cases by paying an additional amount instead of facing penalties.
Income Tax Reforms Introduced in Budget 2026-27
The Income Tax Act, 1961 had been in place for more than 60 years. With numerous amendments added across decades, a change was needed to make compliance easy..
To address this, the Union Budget 2026–27 introduces the Income Tax Act, 2025, which will come into force from 1 April 2026. The new law aims to simplify the tax system by simplifying and introducing changes in today’s modern era. At the same time, the government has kept income tax slabs unchanged and extended the deadline to revise Income Tax Returns up to 31 March of the assessment year, giving taxpayers more time to make corrections.2
Conclusion
The Union Budget of 2026 has proposed various reforms and measures that can help in boosting the economic growth of the country. Whether it is indirect taxation, different sectors of the economy or direct taxation, the budget has proposed major reforms for the upcoming financial year.

While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before
making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
*The above content is subject to the passing of the Finance Bill 2026 in the parliament.

changes.word_original: Grouping IT services under a single category with a common safe harbour margin of 15.5%
changes.pdf_final: ● Grouping IT services under a single category with a common safe harbour margin of 15.5% ● Increase in the safe harbour threshold from ₹300 crore to ₹2,000 crore ● Automated, rule-based approval of safe harbour ● Option to continue safe harbour for five years at the company’s choice ● Faster unilateral APA process with a target timeline of two years, extendable by six months on the tax payer’s request. ● Extension of modified return facilities to associated entities of APA-availing companies ● Tax holidays until 2047 for foreign companies providing cloud services through India- based data centres ● Safe harbour of 15% on cost for related data centre service entities ● Exemption on the global income of non-resident experts for a stay of up to five years under the notified schemes.

changes.pdf_final: Important Policy and Procedural Changes Introduced in Budget 2026–271

changes.word_original: Bharat-VISTAAR and Technology Integration
changes.pdf_final: ● Persons resident outside India will now be allowed to invest in equity shares of listed Indian companies through the Portfolio Investment Scheme (PIS). ● Interest compensation awarded by a Motor Accident Claims Tribunal to an individual will be fully exempt from income tax, and no TDS will apply on such payments. ● The TCS rate on overseas tour packages has been reduced to 2%, replacing the earlier rates of 5% and 20%, with no minimum amount condition. ● TCS under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme for education and medical expenses has been lowered from 5% to 2%. ● TDS on manpower supply services will be applied at a reduced rate of 1% or 2%, depending on the nature of the transaction. ● Small taxpayers will be able to obtain lower or nil TDS deduction certificates through an automated, rule-based system. ● Depositories will be permitted to accept Form 15G and Form 15H even when taxpayers hold securities across multiple companies. ● The deadline for revising income tax returns has been extended from 31 December to 31 March, subject to payment of a nominal fee. ● Individuals filing ITR-1 and ITR-2 will continue to have a due date of 31 July, while non-audit business cases and trusts will be allowed time up to 31 August. ● For the sale of immovable property by a non-resident, TDS will now be deducted and deposited using the resident buyer’s PAN, removing the need for a TAN. ● A one-time, six-month window will be introduced for disclosure of foreign assets below a specified threshold for small taxpayers. ● Taxpayers will be allowed to update their returns even after reassessment proceedings begin by paying an additional 10% tax over the applicable rate. ● The framework granting immunity from penalty and prosecution in cases of underreporting will now also apply to certain cases of misreporting. ● Failure to maintain books of accounts or delay in TDS payment will be decriminalised, reducing prosecution-related burden.

changes.word_original: Integration of AgriStack portals with the ICAR package of agricultural practices.
changes.pdf_final: ● Retrospective immunity from prosecution (effective 1 October 2024) will be provided for non-disclosure of foreign assets other than immovable property, where the total value is below ₹20 lakh. ● Non-residents opting for presumptive taxation will be exempt from Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT). ● A joint committee of the Ministry of Corporate Affairs and CBDT will be formed to align Income Computation and Disclosure Standards (ICDS) with Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS). ● Share buybacks will be taxed as capital gains for all shareholders, while promoters will be subject to an additional buyback tax. ● Set-off of available MAT credit will be allowed up to 25% of the tax liability under the new tax regime. ● MAT is proposed to be treated as a final tax, simplifying compliance. ● Basic Customs Duty will be exempted on 17 specified cancer-related drugs and medicines. ● A single, integrated digital window will be introduced for cargo clearance approvals. ● The Customs Integrated System (CIS) will be implemented over the next two years. ● Taxpayers who wish to settle disputes amicably will be allowed to close cases by paying an additional amount instead of facing penalties.

changes.word_original: Use of AI-enabled systems to support smarter, data-driven farming decisions.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: With numerous amendments added across decades, the law had grown outdated and confusing for many taxpayers, making compliance harder than it needed to be.
changes.pdf_final: With numerous amendments added across decades, a change was needed to make compliance easy..

changes.word_original: To address this, the Union Budget 2026–27 introduces the Income Tax Act, 2025, which will come into force from 1 April 2026 as announced by finance minister today.
changes.pdf_final: To address this, the Union Budget 2026–27 introduces the Income Tax Act, 2025, which will come into force from 1 April 2026.

changes.word_original: At the same time, the government has kept income tax slabs unchanged t and extended the deadline to revise Income Tax Returns up to 31 March of the assessment year, giving taxpayers more time to make corrections
changes.pdf_final: At the same time, the government has kept income tax slabs unchanged and extended the deadline to revise Income Tax Returns up to 31 March of the assessment year, giving taxpayers more time to make corrections.2

changes.word_original: While the budget does not announce any reforms related to life insurance or introduce changes to life insurance taxation, life insurance and health insurance policies continue to attract 0% GST, keeping protection affordable for families.
changes.pdf_final: The Union Budget of 2026 has proposed various reforms and measures that can help in boosting the economic growth of the country.
Whether it is indirect taxation, different sectors of the economy or direct taxation, the budget has proposed major reforms for the upcoming financial year.

changes.word_original: Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content.
changes.pdf_final: Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before
making any financial or investment decisions based on the content.
